author: @AORD72
text: I suspect the duck water storage generation to be horrifically bad. PE =mgh. Let's say 1 cubic meter of water and let's say as 1meter lift you get 2.7 Wh. 65Wh a day, 24kWh a year. At $0.20 of power you save $4.8 a year (that's with 100% efficiency, reality might be $1-2). If the duck structure (to hold a tonne) cost $1000, you will need 208 years of generation to pay it back, reality might be more like 416 years!

author: @TomFarrell-p9z
text:
Collecting energy from solar-wind is all well and good.  There are very serious technical issues with transmitting energy back to Earth with lasers, weapons treaties not-with-standing.  There is the issue of focusing (sidelobes can kill at those energy levels), and tracking through a turbulent atmosphere; thermal blooming, where the beam is strong enough to create its own turbulence; and clouds which, believe it or not, will block the whole thing.  Also, the atmospheric absorption will likely be enough to alter the environment--what some folks like to call climate change.  I'm all for trying to solve the problems, but then I've worked in that industry for the past 25 years.  But don't underestimate the problems.

Another possibility that isn't too far fetched is to use the energy up there, beyond the magnetosphere.  How about autonomous "factories" in orbit producing things that require tremendous amounts of power?  It may be a more practical possibility.

author: @InssiAjaton
text: "5.4 kilowatt-hours" from the wind turbine tree...  In what time? An hour, I assume, but maybe it is in one day?  Or one week?  Or their lifetime?

replies.author: @AORD72
replies.text:
I guess per day. If each turbine is 1 m and 200mm of wind facing blade you get 0.2 m2. With 36 turbines you have a surface area of 7.2 m2.
With air density of 1.2, a wind speed of 4m/s (average city wind speed) and efficiency of 20% you can calculate the power to be 7.2*1.2*4^3*0.2= 110 Watts. So over 24hours you get 2.6 kWh (reality is probably 1.3kW due to cut in/out wind speed). So save you about $0.60 a day, or $189 a year.  If each turbine costs $100 to make in materials and labour, that's $3600 plus the metal frame tree let's say $4000. So payback might take about 21 years, or more realistically 42 years!

replies.author: @AORD72
replies.text: I suspect the duck water storage generation to be horrifically bad. PE =mgh. Let's say 1 cubic meter and let's say as 1meter lift you get 2.7 Wh. 65Wh a day, 24kWh a year. At $0.20 of power you save $4.8 a year (that's with 100% efficiency, reality might be $1-2). If the duck structure (to hold a tonne) cost $1000, you will need 208 years of generation to pay it back, reality might be more like 416 years!

author: @Suzuki_Hiakura
text: Just had an idea for a battery using air in a bag that's underwater. Saw a youtuber use two bags or so and a small air compressor to lift bikes and such from the mud under the lake. Some fo the stuff shot up at incredible speeds... perhaps that would be a decent force to attach to a motor? Would only need to be about 8-11 feet or so (I think was the distance from the bottom). Kinda unrelated, but the video kinda made me think about it and figured people might find it interesting lol

replies.author: @TerrorTubbie666
replies.text:
Yes, I'm pretty sure this would work.
It's like an upsidedown gravitybattery.

replies.author: @TerrorTubbie666
replies.text:
And now you mention this, I'm thinking about this project I recently saw on tv:
They had a kite fly figure-8's (or rather infinitysymbols) and used the difference in pullingforce to generate electricity.
Maybe this could also work with a kite-like construction in a river???
Or in a (tidal) stream in the sea?

replies.author: @Suzuki_Hiakura
replies.text:
@TerrorTubbie666 Would that not be trying to recreate the turbines in dams or the wave generator? 

The battery would rely on buoyancy instead of gravity, which you would only need to lift the bag up or turn over to return to the bottom of the lake. And then you just need to put air back in the bag to restart the process. A small enough tub just needs to release enough air into the bag to expand it it will rise. Unsure how you would implement it without the human element to reset... perhaps a ballon thats sealed that tugs above the bag, so it keeps it upright so the air could be caught again?

replies.author: @Marian_Martin_Morgenstern
replies.text:
Thats actually a thing thats being researched, big Tanks, like giant upside down big drinking glasses or vases, are submerged in lakes and then filled with Air.
The water stream in with quite some force when you let the Air escape again - depending on depth. And that Water can be use to turn a turbine and produce energy

If you use it as the base structure for offshore wind, it would work as a low tech, low maintenance, local battery for that wind turbine, wouldn't it?

Is it supposed to be similar to an alternate dam? Sounds like the water is rushing in, which causes the turbines to rotate... if I am understanding correctly...

replies.author: @russellzauner
replies.text: Speaking of lakes - there are many reservoirs that are covered with black plastic balls to keep bromate formation down, among other things - that's always fascinated me with the opportunities for generation and harvesting both.  I mean, they're always moving a little bit and they're always at least 30% exposed to the sky (or even maybe something that can use the bromate formed in the water, like batteries in the balls that literally pull the bromate and use it for sustaining peak energy storage and transfer).

author: @AnnVole
text: Raising and lowering an entire house is a possible hydraulic-medium energy storage idea due to a neglected "energy source" of high efficiency. I want to raise the house off the foundation so I can replace basement walls with insulation both for the basement and for heat stored in the soil below the basement floor. Moving the weight of the house outside the footprint of the house provides the opportunity to make earthquake movement isolation but also puts the whole house weight on four points (corners of the rectangle house footprint) for possible raising and lowering for energy storage. Wheelchair ramps hide the movement from being noticed (and prevent earthquake damage from an earthquake). Storing water both below the basement and in the attic allows some energy storage via water pressure.
